claimed_contribution: The paper proposes a hierarchical Bayesian recommendation system that can integrate five information sources: a user's expressed preferences, other users' preferences, expert evaluations, item characteristics, and individual characteristics.
It introduces unobserved product heterogeneity into preference modeling by interacting latent movie effects with customer characteristics, in addition to customer-level heterogeneity.
It provides an MCMC estimation approach and evaluates recommendations both when collaborative filtering is feasible and when collaborative filtering cannot make recommendations.
context: The empirical setting is Internet movie recommendation using DEC's EachMovie data. The study evaluates recommendations for existing versus new customers and old versus new movies.
design_and_data: The authors build a regression-based hierarchical Bayesian framework and estimate it with MCMC, with fixed effects for observed movie and user variables and random effects for customer and movie heterogeneity. From EachMovie, they use 2,000 customers and 340 movies (adding expert ratings from external sources), with 56,239 observed ratings out of 680,000 possible. The calibration sample has 10,344 ratings on 228 movies by 986 customers, and four validation sets represent old person/old movie, old person/new movie, new person/old movie, and new person/new movie conditions. They compare a full model against 11 restricted models using log-marginal likelihood, DIC, RMSE, and MAD-based comparisons to published collaborative filtering results and a pooled regression baseline.
main_findings: The full model with both customer and movie heterogeneity plus genre and expert variables had the best performance among 12 models on both log-marginal likelihood and DIC.

Accounting for customer heterogeneity improved predictive performance more than modeling movie heterogeneity alone, and estimates showed substantial cross-customer variation in genre and expert effects.

On holdout prediction, the proposed model generally outperformed restricted variants and reported lower MAD than several published collaborative filtering results, while performance declined and predictions became more conservative as less user/movie information was available.
one_line_summary: The paper develops and tests a hierarchical Bayesian movie recommendation system that integrates multiple information sources and models both customer and movie heterogeneity, yielding stronger predictive performance than restricted alternatives in sparse-data settings.
relation_to_prior_work: extends prior hierarchical Bayesian preference models in marketing by adding explicit unobserved product heterogeneity and applying the framework to Internet recommendation, with empirical comparison to collaborative filtering-style approaches.
research_gap: The paper states that existing collaborative and attribute-based systems each use limited information and have practical weaknesses such as sparse-data problems, inability to handle new items, and limited uncertainty/explanation capabilities. It also notes a lack of published comparisons across these recommendation approaches and against an uncustomized pooled regression baseline.
research_question: How can an Internet recommendation system combine multiple information sources to improve individual-level predictions beyond collaborative or content filtering alone? The paper examines whether a hierarchical Bayesian model that jointly captures customer and product heterogeneity can deliver better movie recommendations across old/new user and old/new item scenarios.
